metadata.title: Build the Faraday Cage: Copper's Skin Depth with Claude
metadata.note: CLI spine: B00 INTRO (ClaudeComposerAsk cold open) · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE (ClaudeCodeBeat, real script) · B04 OUTPUT (Manim: skin depth log-log + MRI markers) · B05 CHANGE (ClaudeComposerAsk, material comparison) · B06 OUTPUT (Manim: 3 materials) · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS (handoff 'Your turn.') · B09 OUTRO (ClaudeTitleOutro @NikBearBrown). One required revision (B05→B06).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Gauss's law forces the field inside a conductor to zero, but only if the copper is thicker than its skin depth. Skin depth equals root of two rho over omega mu zero, shrinking by a factor of three per decade of frequency. At MRI's 64 megahertz copper's skin depth is about 8 micrometers, so one millimeter attenuates RF by over a thousand decibels. Copper's conductivity is 44 times steel's, so the material choice and the spec follow directly from the skin-depth formula.

artifactLines: Gauss's law forces the field inside a conductor to zero, but only if the copper is thicker than its skin depth.
Skin depth equals root of two rho over omega mu zero, shrinking by a factor of three per decade of frequency.
At MRI's 64 megahertz copper's skin depth is about 8 micrometers, so one millimeter attenuates RF by over a thousand decibels.
Copper's conductivity is 44 times steel's, so the material choice and the spec follow directly from the skin-depth formula.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — open on Claude UI, ask shown answered
beats.narration_text: What surprised me is that a single millimeter of copper gives over a thousand decibels of attenuation at the MRI frequency because the skin depth collapses to 8 micrometers. I want to push on the frequency dependence. Can you (1) explain why skin depth falls by a factor of three per decade of frequency; (2) trace how the mu-zero in the denominator makes this genuinely electromagnetic rather than merely resistive; and (3) work out at what frequency a stainless-steel room would match copper's attenuation per millimeter?

props.command: What surprised me is that a single millimeter of copper gives over a thousand decibels of attenuation at the MRI frequency because the skin depth collapses to 8 micrometers. I want to push on the frequency dependence. Can you (1) explain why skin depth falls by a factor of three per decade of frequency; (2) trace how the mu-zero in the denominator makes this genuinely electromagnetic rather than merely resistive; and (3) work out at what frequency a stainless-steel room would match copper's attenuation per millimeter?

beats.role_note: Stakes before the CLI loop — no prompt yet
beats.narration_text: An MRI scanner runs at 64 megahertz. The room is lined with copper — millimeter-thick — to keep RF noise out. Gauss's law guarantees the field inside a conductor is exactly zero. But that zero only holds if the copper is thick enough relative to the skin depth. This script computes the skin depth and the attenuation for any frequency — so you know exactly how much copper you need.
beats.visual_intent: Title card: 'Faraday cage — field inside = 0 (Gauss)' with sub-note 'Only if copper thickness >> skin depth'; no composer yet

build.suggested: suggested prompt: An MRI scanner runs at 64 megahertz. The room is lined with copper — millimeter-thick — to keep RF noise out. Gauss's law guarantees the field inside a conducto

beats.role_note: The actual prompt typed into Claude (ClaudeComposerAsk)
beats.narration_text: Into Claude: write faraday underscore cage dot py. Skin depth delta equals root of two rho over omega mu naught, copper at 1.68 times ten to the negative eight ohm meters. Frequencies log-spaced from one kilohertz to ten gigahertz. Attenuation in dB for a one-millimeter sheet: twenty d over natural log ten, divided by delta. Plot both log-log. Mark 64 megahertz for 1.5 tesla and 128 megahertz for 3 tesla.

props.command:
claude "write faraday_cage.py:
  delta = sqrt(2*rho / (omega*mu_0))   # skin depth
  copper rho = 1.68e-8 Ohm*m
  f = logspace(1e3, 1e10, 500)
  d = 1e-3   # 1 mm sheet
  A_dB = 20/ln(10) * d/delta
  plot delta(f) and A_dB(f) log-log
  mark 64 MHz (1.5T MRI) and 128 MHz (3T MRI)"

beats.role_note: ACTUAL-CODE LAW — real generated source, trimmed to what teaches
beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. Two functions, eight lines. The skin depth formula is one line: root of two rho over omega mu zero. Attenuation is one multiplication: twenty d over the natural log of ten, divided by delta. The mu zero in the denominator is what makes this electromagnetic — it connects resistivity to the magnetic field component. Check the MRI numbers before accepting it.

props.title: faraday_cage.py
props.code:
# faraday_cage.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np
import scipy.constants as c

def skin_depth(f, rho=1.68e-8):        # copper: ρ = 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m
    omega = 2 * np.pi * f
    return np.sqrt(2 * rho / (omega * c.mu_0))

f     = np.logspace(3, 10, 500)        # 1 kHz → 10 GHz
d     = 1e-3                           # 1 mm sheet
delta = skin_depth(f)
A_dB  = 20 / np.log(10) * (d / delta) # attenuation  ← the receipt
props.sparkLine: One formula. Three orders of magnitude.

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot — Manim: two log-log plots; MRI markers animate in
beats.narration_text: Run it. Left: skin depth sweeps from a third of a millimeter at one kilohertz down to 8.4 micrometers at 64 megahertz. Slope is negative one half — every decade of frequency, skin depth shrinks by a factor of three. Right: attenuation for a one-millimeter sheet climbs past a thousand decibels at the MRI frequency. One millimeter of copper. Over a thousand decibels. That is the number behind every RF-shielded room.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: left panel — δ(f) log-log sweeping down, x=log10(freq) 5-10, y=log10(delta) -7 to -4; right panel — A_dB(f) log-log sweeping up, y=log10(A_dB) 0-3; MRI marker at 64 MHz annotated '8.4 μm → 1033 dB/mm' on both panels

beats.narration_text: That gives you copper. Now ask: why not aluminum? Revise: add aluminum at 2.82 times ten to the negative eight ohm meters, and stainless steel 304 at 7.4 times ten to the negative seven, on the same log-log plot. Show the skin depth for each material at 64 megahertz.

props.command:
claude "update faraday_cage.py:
  -> add aluminum (rho=2.82e-8) and stainless steel 304 (rho=7.4e-7)
  -> plot all three skin_depth curves on one log-log axes
  -> at 64 MHz: print delta_um and A_dB for each material"

beats.role_note: OPEN vox slot — Manim: three material curves with 64 MHz comparison
beats.narration_text: Re-run. Three curves. Copper: 8.1 micrometers at 64 megahertz. Aluminum: 10.5 — close. Stainless steel: 54 micrometers — six-and-a-half times deeper. More depth, less attenuation per millimeter. Copper's conductivity is 44 times better than steel. The material choice follows from the skin depth formula. The physics decides the spec.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: three log-log curves (copper red, aluminum blue, steel green) on one axes x=log10(f) 5-10, y=log10(delta) -7 to -2; 64 MHz dashed vertical marker; dots + legend showing '8.1 μm / 10.5 μm / 54.0 μm' at 64 MHz

beats.narration_text: So: delta equals root of two rho over omega mu zero. At 64 megahertz, copper gives 8.4 micrometers. A one-millimeter sheet gives over a thousand decibels of attenuation — six times better than stainless at the same thickness. Claude built the script in one prompt. The physics is two lines. The insight is one formula.
beats.visual_intent: Recap card: formula δ = √(2ρ/ωμ₀) at top; table: copper 8.4 μm / 1033 dB, aluminum 10.5 μm / 823 dB, steel 54 μm / 160 dB; caption 'One prompt. One formula. Every RF-shielded room.'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So: delta equals root of two rho over omega mu zero. At 64 megahertz, copper gives 8.4 micrometers. A one-millimeter sheet gives over a thousand decibels of att

beats.narration_text: Your move. Take this prompt: add a copper mesh with 70 percent fill factor, model effective conductivity as 0.7 times bulk, and show how the skin depth and attenuation compare to solid sheet at both MRI frequencies. Ask Claude to print the crossover point where the mesh drops below solid aluminum.

props.command: claude "update faraday_cage.py: add copper mesh (fill_factor=0.7, rho_eff=rho_Cu/0.7). At 64 MHz and 128 MHz, compare skin_depth and A_dB for: solid copper, copper mesh, solid aluminum. Print the crossover frequency where mesh A_dB drops below solid aluminum A_dB."
